note_data.title: Cell Cycle
keyPoints: Cell cycle is the sequence of events that occurs in a cell leading to its division and the production of two daughter cells
Cell cycle consists of two main phases: Interphase (G1, S, G2) and M phase (Mitosis and Cytokinesis)
G1 phase is the gap phase where cell grows in size and organelles are duplicated
S phase is synthesis phase where DNA replication occurs and chromosome number doubles
G2 phase is second gap phase where proteins necessary for chromosome condensation are produced
M phase includes mitosis (nuclear division) and cytokinesis (cytoplasmic division)
Checkpoints control the cell cycle progression at G1/S, G2/M, and metaphase/anaphase transitions
Cancer cells have defective cell cycle control mechanisms leading to uncontrolled division
Cell cycle duration varies in different cell types - rapidly dividing cells like in bone marrow complete cycle in 24 hours

definitions.definition: The sequence of events that occurs in a cell leading to its division and the production of two daughter cells
The phase of cell cycle during which the cell grows and DNA is replicated, consisting of G1, S and G2 phases
The process of nuclear division in which the nucleus divides to form two nuclei with identical sets of chromosomes
The division of cytoplasm that follows nuclear division during cell division
Control mechanisms that ensure proper progression of cell cycle by checking for DNA damage and proper chromosome attachment

importantQuestions.question: Define cell cycle and name its phases.
importantQuestions.answer: Cell cycle is the sequence of events that occurs in a cell leading to its division and the production of two daughter cells. Its phases are: Interphase (G1, S, G2) and M phase (Mitosis and Cytokinesis).

importantQuestions.question: Describe the phases of cell cycle in detail.
importantQuestions.answer: Cell cycle consists of Interphase and M phase. Interphase has three sub-phases: G1 phase where cell grows and organelles duplicate, S phase where DNA replication occurs, and G2 phase where proteins for chromosome condensation are made. M phase includes mitosis (nuclear division with prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase) and cytokinesis (cytoplasmic division). Checkpoints control progression between phases.

mcqs.question: DNA replication occurs during which phase?
Which of the following is NOT a phase of mitosis?

commonMistakes: Students confuse S phase with synthesis of proteins instead of DNA synthesis
Often mix up the order of mitotic phases - remember PMAT (Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase)
Forget that cytokinesis is separate from mitosis and is part of M phase
Confuse cell cycle with cell division - cell cycle includes both growth and division phases
Don't mention that checkpoints prevent damaged cells from dividing
examTips: This chapter usually has 1-2 MCQs and 1 short question in every BSEK paper
Always draw and label the cell cycle diagram - it appears frequently in exams
Memorize exact definitions as they appear in STBB textbook
Practice drawing mitosis phases diagram as it's commonly asked
Remember checkpoint locations: G1/S, G2/M, and metaphase/anaphase
note_data.keyDiagram: Cell cycle diagram showing circular representation with Interphase (G1, S, G2 phases) taking major portion and M phase (Mitosis + Cytokinesis) as smaller section, with checkpoints marked at transitions between phases
